title: The Ten Percent Solution

jokes.text: Cockroach at 10:00. Oh, my God! That's the biggest cockroach I've ever seen!
jokes.setup: Military-style alert about a cockroach position
jokes.punchline: Treating a bug like a major threat with over-the-top military precision
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the cockroach running gag. Louie's obsessiveness established early with military language.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: We have a Code Red! I repeat, Code Red! There's a cockroach in sector 7!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Should we call in the exterminator?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Negative! This is a full tactical operation. I want checkpoints at every corner of the garage. Nobody gets in or out without proper decontamination!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown driver
dialogue.line: Louie, it's just a bug.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Just a bug?! That insect could be carrying classified intelligence! We treat this with the utmost military precision or New York City falls!

jokes.text: That's a man-eater.
jokes.setup: Previous establishment of 'biggest cockroach'
jokes.punchline: Escalating to predatory language about a bug
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character voice. Louie's paranoia and dramatic flair shine through.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think that's a big cockroach? That ain't nothing. I seen cockroaches in this garage so big, they got their own union rep.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I seen one cockroach so big, it mugged a pigeon for its bread crumbs.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: There's a cockroach in the back room right now that's got a contract with Orkin. He's their top salesman.

jokes.text: I hit him dead center with everything I had, and he just smiled at me.
jokes.setup: Expectation that hitting a cockroach would kill it
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing the roach as smugly invincible
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist escalation. The image of a cockroach 'smiling' after being hit is both vivid and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I hit that cockroach with a newspaper and it just looked at me like, 'Is that all you got, pal?'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The thing dusted itself off and walked away. Didn't even have the courtesy to die.

jokes.text: But he'll be back.
jokes.setup: Previous cockroach battle
jokes.punchline: Ominous movie villain delivery about a bug
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie paranoia. Treating a cockroach like Terminator with dramatic foreboding.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You thought you could defeat me, but I am eternal. I will return again and again, more powerful each time. You cannot stop me.

jokes.text: Who am I calling? / You mean, you weren't going to use the phone? / No, I was going to the bathroom.
jokes.setup: Jim offering to make a short call
jokes.punchline: Reveal that Jim was just going to bathroom, not using phone
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection showcasing Jim's sweet but confused personality.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm just going to make a short call.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Sure, go ahead.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I meant I'm going to the bathroom.

jokes.text: Oh, Bob, Bob, come on. It's not the last job you're going to lose.
jokes.setup: Tony trying to be supportive about Bobby's audition
jokes.punchline: Accidentally pessimistic 'encouragement'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony - well-meaning but saying exactly the wrong thing. Great character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're gonna be great, Bobby. I mean, you might not get the part, but you'll definitely go in there and read the lines.

jokes.text: Let him hear the negative things after he loses it.
jokes.setup: Trying to correct Tony's pessimism
jokes.punchline: Accidentally even more brutally pessimistic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the accidental pessimism. Shows even the 'smart' character can fumble.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Tony, you need to stop being so negative. Look on the bright side for once.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The bright side? Elaine, there is no bright side. We're all just slowly dying, our dreams are illusions, and nothing we do matters.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: See? That's exactly what I'm talking about. You know what? At least you have a job. At least you have your health.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: For now. But eventually my health will fail, I'll lose my job, and I'll spend my final years alone in a rent-controlled apartment, watching my pension disappear.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, when you put it that way, being pessimistic doesn't seem so bad.

jokes.text: First, I would like to order a tossed gravlitee... and a fried yufnish in the basket... and a profnika with everything.
jokes.setup: Latka ordering from his native restaurant
jokes.punchline: Made-up foreign food names that sound absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka foreign language humor. Character-specific but relatively predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I'll have the... uh... Bleviticus Maximus with a side of Florbengarten.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, from my country. The Bleviticus Maximus. And maybe some Schmendrickle sauce on top.

jokes.text: Just give me the number seven.
jokes.setup: Long elaborate foreign food order
jokes.punchline: Could have just ordered a combo number
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to the elaborate setup. Great economy in the punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Could have just ordered a combo number

jokes.text: Banta, sometimes I wish you were smarter just so you'd know how dumb you are.
jokes.setup: Tony's impatient question about the phone call
jokes.punchline: Perfectly crafted insult with circular logic
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie insult with brilliant structure. The circular logic makes it both cutting and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Who is it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's for you.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Well, who is it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: If I knew who it was, I would've told you who it was. The fact that I don't know who it is is why I'm telling you it's for you, so you can find out who it is. If you don't want to find out who it is, then it's not for you.

jokes.text: It's a handicap to be classically handsome. Don't I know it?
jokes.setup: Bobby explaining why good looks hurt his career
jokes.punchline: Alex sarcastically agreeing he suffers from same 'problem'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Alex's dry delivery of self-deprecating humor works well.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You see, being this good-looking, it's actually a curse. People don't take me seriously as an actor. They just see the face.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know exactly what you mean. It's the same problem I have.

jokes.text: Go ahead and say it, Bobby.
jokes.setup: Bobby describes the ideal casting type
jokes.punchline: Tony realizes and encourages Bobby to name him
jokes.explanation: Good setup to payoff, shows Tony's awareness but also his vanity

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know what? I think I finally figured out what kind of part I'm right for.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm the guy. You know, the guy who comes in, says one line, gets a big laugh, and leaves.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Bobby, that's perfect for you!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You should go for it. You should definitely go for that.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah! In fact, they should name that part after you.

jokes.text: Thank God... It suffered long enough.
jokes.setup: Bobby declaring his acting career dead
jokes.punchline: Alex treating it like putting a sick animal out of misery
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark and economical. Alex's deadpan delivery makes this cutting and memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: My acting career is dead.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, we'd better put it out of its misery.

jokes.text: Well, uh, how do you feel about answering? I could make up a question, you could answer it.
jokes.setup: Bobby not wanting to talk
jokes.punchline: Tony's bizarre solution to conversation
jokes.explanation: Sweet Tony logic. Shows his well-meaning but confused approach to problems.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Bobby, you gotta talk to me. What's wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I don't wanna talk about it, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is? You hold everything inside. You need to express yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I said I don't wanna talk!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Alright, alright. So don't talk. But you gotta communicate somehow. I got an idea - you grunt once for yes, twice for no.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: See? You're talking already! My system works!

jokes.text: I was the guy that woke him up. Hey, Bobby, you think it's easy waking a guy up after he's slept 40 years?
jokes.setup: Tony's theatrical experience in Rip Van Winkle
jokes.punchline: Taking the tiny role seriously with method acting approach
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony - taking a throwaway role seriously. Great character specificity and absurd logic.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm playing Rip Van Winkle's friend. It's a small part, but I'm approaching it with total method acting. I've been sleeping for three weeks to prepare.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, that's just you being lazy.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, it's character research. Rip sleeps for twenty years, so my character needs to understand the sleep process intimately.

jokes.text: I don't think I want your agent. And why not? I mean, he hasn't exactly made you a star, you know.
jokes.setup: Bobby offering to call his agent for Tony
jokes.punchline: Tony's brutally honest assessment of Bobby's career
jokes.explanation: Tony's innocent cruelty. He doesn't mean to hurt but states obvious truth.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'll call my agent, he can represent you too.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Bobby, your agent doesn't represent you. Your agent is a guy who answers a phone in a hallway.

jokes.text: A girl I once dated said I was an Italian Sylvester Stallone. Yeah, I think the world is waiting for one of those.
jokes.setup: Tony's confidence about his looks
jokes.punchline: Bobby's sarcastic response about market saturation
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic comeback. Bobby's wit cutting through Tony's delusion.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I got the looks. Women go crazy for me.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, Tony. They go crazy. Crazy to get away from you. Besides, even if you looked like a movie star, there's a whole city full of guys who look like movie stars. You'd be competing with thousands of them.

jokes.text: Cat on a Hot Tin Roof. I seen the Disney version.
jokes.setup: Tony studying serious Tennessee Williams play
jokes.punchline: Confusing it with something Disney would never make
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment. Perfect economy showing Tony's cultural confusion in one line.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: This Tennessee Williams, he's heavy stuff. 'A Streetcar Named Desire'... 'Cat on a Hot Tin Roof'... 'The Glass Menagerie'...

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, Disney should make a musical out of one of these.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: 'Cinderella,' but with Blanche DuBois.

jokes.text: If he could see over a dashboard, I'd have this one driving a cab.
jokes.setup: Defending cockroach intelligence
jokes.punchline: Practical consideration of employing the roach
jokes.explanation: Great callback with escalation. Louie's respect for the roach reaches absurd business consideration.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, I'd hire a cockroach. They're loyal, they work cheap, and they don't complain.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Louie, a cockroach can't drive a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So? Half my drivers can't either. At least the roach wouldn't call in sick.

jokes.text: when I catch this one, I'm gonna fix you up with it.
jokes.setup: Elaine teasing Louie about the cockroach
jokes.punchline: Louie threatening to set her up on a date with a bug
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific threat. Louie's petty revenge fantasy.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You think you're so funny? I'm gonna set you up on a date with that cockroach.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh please, Louie, he's not even my type.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Not your type? He's got six legs, he can carry fifty times his own weight, and he doesn't talk back. He's perfect for you!

jokes.text: Three poses for 50 cents.
jokes.setup: Tony showing off his headshots
jokes.punchline: Revealing the bargain-basement price for professional photos
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's naivety about the business. The price point reveals his amateur status.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Look at these headshots! Professional quality, real photographer, studio lighting.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know how much I paid? Five bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The guy said, 'Tony, I've never charged so little.' I said, 'Why?' He said, 'Because I've never done it before.'

jokes.text: could you to be my agent, too?
jokes.setup: Latka overhearing Tony's acting lesson
jokes.punchline: Immediately wanting representation despite no context
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Latka's eagerness to join anything he doesn't understand.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I want agent! I want agent!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Latka, what are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You say you need agent for acting. I do acting!

jokes.text: [Latka's emotional performance about destroyed village]
jokes.setup: Latka describing a tragic movie scene from his village
jokes.punchline: Extended emotional performance that's simultaneously moving and ridiculous
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy showcase for Latka. Long setup but pays off with genuine emotion mixed with absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: In my village, there was a man who loved a woman, but she was promised to another. One day, he stood at the edge of the cliff and he cried out her name... 'MARGAAAAARET!' And the wind, it carried his voice across the mountains, and she heard it, and she knew... she knew he would never see her again. And he jumped.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But as he was falling, a large bird—a very large bird, like an eagle made of clouds—it caught him! And carried him away to a magical castle where time had no meaning, and he lived forever, watching her from the towers, always watching...

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: *breaks down* She never knew. She waited by the window every night of her life. Every night! And he was right there... in the castle made of sky... watching her... waiting...

jokes.text: I see that we're presently negotiating for a part in the new Jane Fonda film, which could tie him up during that period.
jokes.setup: Bobby pretending to be a professional agent
jokes.punchline: Completely fabricated high-profile negotiation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Bobby's fake agent persona. Shows his desperation to sound professional.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Listen, I just closed a deal for my client with Steven Spielberg. We're talking eight figures, full creative control, the works.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, Spielberg was nervous at first, but once I explained my vision for the project, he just signed right there on the spot.

jokes.text: I can always work out something with Jane.
jokes.setup: Previous Jane Fonda fabrication
jokes.punchline: Doubling down with casual familiarity
jokes.explanation: Nice callback with escalation. 'Jane' familiarity shows Bobby going deeper into the lie.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah, Jane Fonda and I, we go way back. We work out together at the same gym.

jokes.text: No, but thanks for asking.
jokes.setup: Casting director asking Tony to tell them about himself
jokes.punchline: Politely declining with no awareness it's not optional
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony moment. Complete misunderstanding of audition process with polite delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: Tell me about yourself.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very nice of you to ask, but I'd rather not. Thank you though.

jokes.text: I'll give it a shot.
jokes.setup: Casting director describes the role as eager, innocent, and slow
jokes.punchline: Tony acts like it's a challenge rather than just being himself
jokes.explanation: Ironic that Tony needs to 'act' to be exactly what he already is

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: We need someone eager, innocent, and slow.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Eager, innocent, and slow? That's a tough one. Let me see if I can do it.

jokes.text: Yeah, okay. / Me, too.
jokes.setup: Bobby delivering intense emotional monologue as scene partner
jokes.punchline: Tony's completely inadequate responses to dramatic material
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy. Tony's casual responses to intense emotional work perfectly show his limitations.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I can't go on like this anymore. Every day I wake up and the pain is unbearable. My life has become a prison of regret and broken dreams. I don't know how much longer I can endure this suffering.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, my shift starts in ten minutes.

jokes.text: Me, too.
jokes.setup: Another emotional speech from Bobby about being glad they can talk
jokes.punchline: Tony's second inadequate two-word response
jokes.explanation: Good callback to previous inadequate response, escalates the cringe

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's nice.

jokes.text: After the reading that he just gave you still want him. / Well, it's a small part.
jokes.setup: Bobby's incredulous reaction to Tony being hired
jokes.punchline: Casting director's matter-of-fact dismissal of acting ability
jokes.explanation: Good commentary on Hollywood priorities. The casual dismissal of talent is both funny and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Wait, you're hiring Tony as an actor? Tony can't act!

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: Can't act? Half the people in this town can't act. That's not a requirement.

jokes.text: Does he work for his ten percent or what?
jokes.setup: Bobby's angry rant about hiring Tony
jokes.punchline: Casting director impressed by Bobby's 'agent' performance
jokes.explanation: Nice ironic twist. Bobby's genuine outrage mistaken for aggressive representation.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know, I'm tired of this! Tony's all wrong for this part. He can't act, he can't remember lines, and frankly, he's a liability!

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: Wait, wait... who are you?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm his agent! And let me tell you, if you hire this guy, you're making a huge mistake. He's difficult, he's unreliable, and—

dialogue.speaker: Casting Director
dialogue.line: Hold on. You're brilliant! That passion, that conviction! How would you like to read for the lead?

jokes.text: I watched George C. Scott eat a jelly doughnut. Then they fired me.
jokes.setup: Tony describing his first day as an actor
jokes.punchline: Treating celebrity sighting and career disaster as equal events
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony perspective. Equal weight given to mundane celebrity encounter and career failure.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I saw the most incredible thing today. I was walking down the street, and I saw Al Pacino. Al Pacino! Right there! I almost fainted.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: And then I got home and found out I didn't get the part. The callback I've been waiting for all week—they gave it to someone else.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: But you know what? I'm not even upset. I saw Al Pacino. That's a win.

jokes.text: He used words like 'wrong interpretation' and 'artistic differences,' 'over my dead body...'
jokes.setup: Tony explaining why he was fired
jokes.punchline: Escalating from polite industry terms to outright hostility
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure. Professional rejection terms escalating to personal animosity.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: They said I was 'let go.' Then they said I was 'terminated.' Then they said I was 'released from my obligations.' Finally, the boss just looked me in the eye and said, 'You're fired, you bum! Get outta here!'

jokes.text: Just 'cause I got canned. / Wow.
jokes.setup: Bobby's elaborate speech about Tony inspiring other actors
jokes.punchline: Tony's simple summary followed by impressed response
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast. Tony's simple perspective vs Bobby's overwrought analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, you've been an inspiration to so many actors in this city. The way you carry yourself, the dignity, the commitment to your craft. Guys come up to me all the time and say, 'Bobby, how does Tony do it?' You've changed lives, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I drive a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Exactly! See? That's what I'm talking about!

jokes.text: But I got to tell you something, I hope they don't expect me to bail them out every time.
jokes.setup: Tony being told his failure will inspire other actors
jokes.punchline: Taking on responsibility for other actors' careers through failure
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy. Tony completely misunderstanding his role as inspiration, with perfect logic.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that my failure is going to inspire other actors?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Great. Now I'm responsible for their careers too.

jokes.text: I put hamburger meat on the end of this. When he nibbles... Yank his head off.
jokes.setup: Louie's elaborate cockroach hunting setup
jokes.punchline: Violent graphic plan for bug murder
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Louie's cockroach obsession. Graphic violence for comedy effect.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: I got a plan to get rid of these cockroaches once and for all. First, I'm gonna dust the entire place with this industrial-strength pesticide. Then I'm gonna seal up every crack and crevice with caulking. After that, I'm gonna bring in a guy with a flamethrower.

jokes.text: He's got hairy legs. He's got bloodshot eyes, Rieger.
jokes.setup: Defending his cockroach obsession
jokes.punchline: Anthropomorphizing the roach with human characteristics
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency. Louie's detailed description makes the roach almost human.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's wrong with you people? This roach, he's got feelings. He's got dreams. He wants things out of life just like you do.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Last night I saw him looking at a picture of a lady roach. He's in love! He's got hopes, he's got aspirations.

jokes.text: He's got hairy legs. He's got bloodshot eyes, Rieger.
jokes.setup: Louie insists this isn't just any roach
jokes.punchline: Describes it with increasingly human characteristics
jokes.explanation: Continues the anthropomorphization of the cockroach, good character obsession

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This isn't just any roach. This roach has character. This roach has dignity. This roach has been to night school.

tmdb_overview: Bobby is fed up after losing an acting gig to an inferior actor with the right look, which he explains to be like Tony. Tony decides he wants to capitalize on this and give acting a try, and asks Bobby to be his manager. Reluctantly, Bobby agrees and soon, despite a terrible audition, Tony gets a major role in a movie. Bobby is soon regretting his decision to take Tony under his wing, though, since he's resentful that Tony (whose acting is not improving, despite Bobby's coaching) could get a part so easily. Bobby feels vinidicated, however, when Tony is eventually cut from the film because of his lack of skills.
insight: Escalation-driven character comedy sustains 79.9 score despite no landmark jokes.
